title: Hojoki: An Account of My Hut
description: A Buddhist meditation on impermanence after earthquake, fire, and famine: the original tiny-house manifesto.
introduction: After witnessing fire, whirlwind, earthquake, and famine devastate Kyoto, Kamo no Chomei withdrew to a ten-foot-square hut in the mountains. His Hojoki (1212) begins with the most famous opening line in Japanese prose after the Pillow Book — a meditation on impermanence that became the touchstone of Buddhist renunciation literature.

translation.original_text: ゆく河の流れは絶えずして、しかももとの水にあらず。
translation.text: The flowing river never stops, and yet the water never stays the same.

translation.original_text: 世の中にある人と栖と、又かくのごとし。
translation.text: So it is with people and their dwellings in this world.
